optionRationalesZh.a: 正确。它完整概括了讲话的两个核心面向：机制和功能。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。讲话确实提到在矿区种植这些植物，但重点不是矿业公司改用什么工具或肥料。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。讲话说镍在植物氮循环中重要，但没有说它是“控制所有植物生长的主要营养”。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。盐分例子只是类比，用来帮助解释适应功能，不是说所有耐旱植物都比盐含量更富金属。

public.prompt: According to the talk, why are these plants used in abandoned nickel mines?

options.text: They rapidly replace all other vegetation with metal-rich shrubs
They remove leftover nickel from the soil and reduce toxic leakage into waterways
They convert nickel directly into plant sugars for nearby crops
They make it unnecessary to process any mined ore afterward

evidence.quote: these plants are actually planted in abandoned nickel mines to take the leftover nickel out of the soil
which keeps the toxic metals from leaching into waterways and makes the land viable for other uses like farming.

private.explanationZh: 这是一道细节题。讲话明确给出两个用途：第一，把土壤里残留的镍吸出来；第二，防止有毒金属渗入水道，并让土地重新变得可用于农业等用途。答案必须同时抓住“去除残留金属”和“减少环境危害”这两个点。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。讲话没有说它们的作用是排挤其他植物。
optionRationalesZh.b: 正确。它准确复述了矿区种植这些植物的环境修复作用。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。没有提到把镍转化成糖，这不符合植物生理讨论内容。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。讲话反而说这些植物之后还可以被收割和处理来提取金属，不是完全不再需要处理。

public.prompt: Why does the speaker bring up salt-hoarding plants?

options.text: To show that salt is chemically identical to nickel in plant cells
To argue that drought is the only reason plants ever hoard metals
To suggest that storing a normally harmful substance can still serve an adaptive purpose
To explain why nickel directly improves photosynthesis in all leaves

evidence.quote: Maybe other hoarders can give us a clue, like the salt-loving plants that use this same mechanism to hoard salt, another nutrient that generally kills plants at high concentrations.
As it turns out, all that hoarded salt attracts more water from the soil

private.explanationZh: 这里的salt-loving plants是一个类比。说话者先提出疑问：金属囤积植物为什么要费能量吸这么多镍？随后用“囤盐植物”说明，一个通常高浓度有害的物质也可能被生物利用成适应优势。因此这个例子是在启发听众思考“囤积有害物质也可能有功能”，为后面提出防御解释做铺垫。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。讲话没有说盐和镍在化学上相同，只说它们都可能在高浓度下有害。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。例子只是展示一种可能机制，不是说金属囤积唯一为了抗旱。
optionRationalesZh.c: 正确。它准确表达了该例子的修辞作用：用平行案例引出适应性解释。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。讲话前面恰恰说过过量镍会伤害叶绿素，而不是普遍提高光合作用。

public.prompt: What advantage do scientists think nickel hoarding may give these plants?

options.text: It may allow the plants to grow faster than all surrounding species
It may let the plants store more sunlight in their roots during winter
It may make the plants independent of water from the soil
It may make the plants poisonous to herbivores without forcing them to manufacture their own toxins

evidence.quote: Scientists believe these super metal plants are (perhaps unsurprisingly) super toxic to some herbivores, which learn to avoid eating them.
So plants that can take up toxic metals - and survive - become toxic themselves, without having to expend energy to concoct their own defensive toxins.

private.explanationZh: 根据结尾的解释，这些植物的潜在优势不是更快生长，而是“借金属之毒来自卫”。如果它们能把有毒金属储存在体内，同时自己又能存活，就可能让食草动物避开它们，从而省去合成复杂化学毒素的能量成本。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。讲话没有说金属囤积让它们生长更快；前文反而强调过量镍通常有毒。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。没有提到把阳光储存在根里，这完全脱离文本。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。盐囤积和吸水有关，但这里讨论的是镍囤积的防御作用，不是完全脱离土壤水分。
optionRationalesZh.d: 正确。两处证据共同说明：对食草动物有毒，而且不必自己制造防御毒素。

reasonsZh.minute-earth-234-q01: 整段都在解释这种植物如何主动吸收并储存大量镍，以及这样做可能带来的防御好处，因此主旨是镍富集机制和潜在优势。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-234-q02: 转录明确说它们被种在废弃镍矿里，是为了把剩余镍从土壤中带走，防止有毒金属渗入水道。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-234-q03: 提到囤盐植物，是为了说明即使某种物质高浓度通常有害，把它储存起来也可能形成适应性用途，从而为后面对镍囤积功能的推测做铺垫。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-234-q04: 结尾说科学家认为这些富金属植物会对部分食草动物非常有毒，使其学会避食，因此优势是借金属让自己有毒，而不必自己合成防御毒素。

public.prompt: What conclusion does the speaker reach about birds that avoid raising their own young?

options.text: Their strategy is not clearly better; it mainly shifts parenting costs into different forms of effort
Their strategy is always more efficient because they lay more eggs than other birds
Their strategy fails only because host birds are usually larger and stronger
Their strategy works best when they cooperate with warblers to share nests

evidence.quote: trying to boost your own reproductive success by getting someone else to raise your babies - either through trickery or force - seems unfair; but the question is, does it work?
So what seems like taking the easy way out is really just a different way of spending your time and energy

private.explanationZh: 讲话一开始提出核心问题：把育儿工作甩给别的鸟，真的划算吗？后面分析布谷鸟和牛鹂都要为这种策略付出额外时间和精力，最后得出的结论是：这不是“更轻松”的捷径，而只是把成本转移成别的劳动形式，而且结果不一定更好。

optionRationalesZh.a: 正确。它准确概括了全文结论：不是明显更优，而是成本结构不同。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。虽然某些“作弊者”会下更多蛋，但讲话明确说这并不一定带来更多存活后代。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。讲话没有把失败原因归结为宿主更大更强，而是强调侦察、监视和惩罚等成本。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。文本讲的是寄生和威胁，不是合作育雏。

public.prompt: Why do cuckoos have to keep looking for additional nests?

options.text: Because they prefer to place each egg in a different species for variety
Because many of their eggs are detected and thrown out by host birds
Because their chicks cannot survive unless multiple hosts feed them
Because predators destroy most of the nests they choose before any eggs hatch

evidence.quote: since about half of the eggs they lay get noticed and dumped anyway, they have to scope out even more nests and lay even more eggs

private.explanationZh: 这是一道直接细节题。说话者明确指出，大约一半的布谷鸟蛋会被宿主发现并扔掉，因此它们不得不继续寻找更多巢、下更多蛋，以弥补损失。关键不是“喜欢多样化”或“天敌破坏”，而是寄生成功率不高。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。讲话没有说它们为了“多样性”而这么做。
optionRationalesZh.b: 正确。它直接对应原文对布谷鸟额外侦察成本的解释。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。文本没有说布谷鸟雏鸟必须依赖多个宿主共同喂养。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。这里说的是宿主识别并丢弃蛋，不是大多数巢先被捕食者毁掉。

public.prompt: Why does the speaker repeatedly describe these strategies in terms of time and energy?

options.text: To prove that birds understand economics better than humans do
To argue that nest-building requires no skill compared with egg-laying
To show that what looks like cheating still carries substantial costs
To suggest that host birds eventually benefit from raising extra chicks

evidence.quote: They simply lay their eggs in any nests they please, but then have to spend a lot of time and energy checking to make sure the host parents are taking good care of the cowbird babies - and punishing those who don't comply.
So what seems like taking the easy way out is really just a different way of spending your time and energy

private.explanationZh: 说话者不断用“time and energy”来衡量，不是为了把故事讲得更抽象，而是为了纠正一种直觉误解：寄生别人的育儿劳动看上去像偷懒，但实际上这种策略也有高昂成本，比如监视宿主、寻找新巢、实施惩罚等。这个框架帮助听众把“作弊”看成成本重分配。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。讲话没有拟人化到说鸟比人更懂经济学。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。说话者没有贬低筑巢技能，也没有说筑巢“无需技巧”。
optionRationalesZh.c: 正确。它准确说明这种反复表述的功能：揭示作弊策略并不免费。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。讲话没有提出宿主会因为多养一只雏鸟而受益，恰恰相反，宿主可能受害。

public.prompt: What problem does the speaker predict if cuckoos and cowbirds became much more successful than their hosts?

options.text: They would have to evolve smaller eggs to fit into warbler nests
They would stop laying eggs and switch to live birth instead
They would lose the ability to recognize host species over time
They could outnumber the birds that actually build nests, undermining the system they depend on

evidence.quote: each breeding season, warblers have an average of four surviving chicks, cowbirds only end up with an average of three
Then they'd outnumber their hosts, who are the only one of the three that even build nests in the first place.

private.explanationZh: 结尾的推论是一个生态依赖关系问题。作弊者之所以能存在，是因为有宿主负责筑巢和抚育。如果它们的繁殖成功远超宿主，宿主数量反而可能不够，整个寄生系统赖以存在的“基础设施”就会被削弱。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。讲话没有把未来问题归结为蛋大小。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。没有任何关于生殖方式改变的讨论，这是荒谬扩展。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。文本没有预测识别能力退化，重点是数量结构失衡。
optionRationalesZh.d: 正确。它准确抓住 speaker 的逻辑：寄生者依赖宿主，若寄生者过多会破坏自身依赖条件。

reasonsZh.minute-earth-235-q01: 讲者结论是这种“作弊”并不更好，只是把时间和能量花在别的地方，而且未必带来更多后代，所以 a 最符合总结。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-235-q02: 文中直接说大约一半布谷鸟蛋会被宿主发现并扔掉，因此它们必须继续寻找更多巢并下更多蛋。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-235-q03: 反复强调时间和能量，是为了说明看似占便宜的寄生育雏策略其实也有高成本，不是白赚。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-235-q04: 最后明确说如果它们后代更多，可能会超过宿主数量；而宿主才是会筑巢的那一方，这会破坏它们赖以生存的体系。

public.prompt: What is the main point of the talk?

options.text: Amateur naturalists now discover many new species because academic scientists face constraints while tools and information have become more accessible
Professional scientists have stopped studying species because most new organisms are too large to classify
New species are mainly discovered in museums because fieldwork is no longer useful
Genome sequencing has made traditional species description unnecessary

evidence.quote: All around the world, scientists are taking a back seat to amateur taxonomists when it comes to discovering new species.
the increasing demands of grants and career advancement mean that scientists need to publish about twice as much as they did twenty years ago.
the recent digitization of scientific literature and taxonomic guides has allowed anyone with enough interest to become an expert in their preferred branch of the tree of life.

private.explanationZh: 主旨不是简单说“业余爱好者很厉害”，而是解释一种结构性变化：一方面，职业科学家受到发表和职业压力限制，难以投入长时间做物种描述；另一方面，数字化文献、指南和更易获得的设备让业余者更容易成长为专家。因此，很多新物种发现工作转移到了业余群体。

optionRationalesZh.a: 正确。它同时包含了讲话的结果和两方面原因，最完整。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。职业科学家并没有“停止研究”，只是受到时间与职业压力限制。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。讲话提到博物馆积压标本，但并未说野外工作已无用。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。基因测序是辅助条件之一，不代表传统描述步骤已经不必要。

public.prompt: According to the talk, what does discovering a new species require besides collecting a specimen?

options.text: Giving it a name immediately and then waiting for another scientist to confirm it later
Studying and describing it carefully, ruling out known variants, and writing up the case for a journal
Finding at least three separate populations on different continents
Obtaining government funding before consulting any experts

evidence.quote: "discovering" a species doesn't just involve collecting a new specimen
and then - and only then - putting all that together and writing it up for an academic journal.

private.explanationZh: 这道题考查对“discovering”定义的把握。说话者特意强调，发现新物种远不止采到一个样本，还包括细致研究、排除其只是已知物种变体的可能，并最终写成学术论文。也就是说，真正的“发现”包含完整的分类学论证过程。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。讲话没有说可以先随便命名再等别人确认，反而强调前期论证必须充分。
optionRationalesZh.b: 正确。它把讲话中列出的关键步骤都概括到了。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。文本没有提出跨洲三地这样的硬性要求。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。讲话提到职业科学家受经费和职业压力影响，但并未说先拿政府资助是发现新物种的步骤。

public.prompt: What can be inferred about the effect of digitized literature and better equipment on amateurs?

options.text: They have removed the need for amateurs to compare specimens with existing research
They have made museum collections irrelevant to species discovery
They have made it more realistic for amateurs to develop expertise and produce evidence strong enough for species identification
They have caused scientists to stop sharing specimens with nonprofessionals

evidence.quote: the recent digitization of scientific literature and taxonomic guides has allowed anyone with enough interest to become an expert in their preferred branch of the tree of life.

as quality cameras and microscopes - and even genome sequencing - have become more accessible, amateurs can now provide the necessary evidence that their specimen is distinct enough to be a new species.

private.explanationZh: 这题要根据两处信息综合推断：数字化文献和分类指南帮助业余者积累知识，易获得的相机、显微镜甚至测序技术帮助他们提供证据。二者结合起来，意味着业余者如今不仅能“爱好”，还更有可能达到正式识别新物种所需的专业水平。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。讲话没有说不再需要和既有研究比较；相反，这仍是发现过程的重要部分。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。博物馆收藏仍然重要，甚至有大量待处理标本。
optionRationalesZh.c: 正确。它准确整合了“成为专家”和“提供证据”这两方面影响。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。结尾反而说有专家愿意把积压标本拿出来让别人去“发现”。

public.prompt: Why does the speaker end by saying there is a "busload of eager amateur experts ready to take the wheel"?

options.text: To argue that transportation workers are the best-trained taxonomists
To suggest that museums should send all specimens to public schools by bus
To show that species discovery is becoming easier because field sites are closer to cities
To return to the opening bus-driver example and emphasize that amateurs are ready to lead more discovery work

evidence.quote: the mollusc-ologist with a big bivalve backlog is happy to hand out specimens so that someone else can "discover" them.
And - luckily for science - there's a busload of eager amateur experts ready to take the wheel.

private.explanationZh: 这句结尾有明显的修辞回环。开头提到一位来自比利时的公交车司机发现了许多海螺新种；结尾再用“busload”“take the wheel”呼应这个例子，强调的不是真有一车人去开车，而是大量热心而有能力的业余专家已经准备接手部分发现工作。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。讲话没有说公交从业者天生最适合做分类学。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。文本没有建议用巴士把标本送去学校，这是无根据联想。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。结尾重点不是地点变近，而是人力结构变化与业余者的积极性。
optionRationalesZh.d: 正确。它既解释了双关/呼应作用，也抓住了“amateurs are ready”这一结尾强调点。

reasonsZh.minute-earth-236-q01: 全文核心是：业余爱好者如今发现了大量新物种，因为学术界受发表压力和时间限制，而文献数字化与设备普及又让业余者更容易成为专家并提供证据。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-236-q02: 转录清楚列出：发现新物种不只是采样，还要详细研究描述、排除只是已知物种变体的可能，并写成学术论文。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-236-q03: 文中说数字化文献和分类指南让任何有兴趣的人都可能成为某类生物专家，而更易获得的相机、显微镜、测序也让他们能拿出足够证据。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-236-q04: 结尾“busload...take the wheel”明显回扣开头那位发现许多新蜗牛种的公交司机，用比喻强调业余专家已经准备好承担更多发现工作。

public.prompt: What is the main focus of the talk?

options.text: How lightning is generated inside ordinary thunderstorms
How firestorms form, persist, and sometimes spread to new areas
Why wildfire smoke always leads to heavy rain
How Australian weather patterns prevent most forest fires

evidence.quote: However, you can use that very same recipe to cook up a special kind of thunderstorm that can seem never-ending: a firestorm.

private.explanationZh: 讲者先从普通雷暴的形成说起，随后转向“特殊版本”的火风暴，重点解释它怎样形成、为什么能持续更久，以及在某些情况下如何把火带到别处。因此主旨不是单独讲闪电、降雨或澳大利亚个案，而是整体说明火风暴的机制与后果。

optionRationalesZh.a: 这个选项只抓住了前面对普通雷暴的部分说明，不是整段内容的核心。
optionRationalesZh.b: 正确。该选项概括了火风暴的形成、持续和扩散三个核心层面，最符合全文主旨。
optionRationalesZh.c: 文中提到火风暴可能产生带烟灰的雨，但并没有说野火烟雾“总会”导致大雨。
optionRationalesZh.d: 澳大利亚黑色星期六只是结尾举的实例，不是全文主要讨论目标。

public.prompt: Why does the rain from a firestorm usually fail to put out the fire below?

options.text: The clouds release too little moisture to reach the ground
Strong winds push the rain away from the burning area
The intense heat evaporates the rain before it reaches the blaze
The soot in the rain makes the flames burn faster

evidence.quote: the fire's intense heat usually causes any rain to evaporate before it hits the blaze below.

private.explanationZh: 讲者明确解释说，火本身的高温通常会让降下来的雨在接触地面火焰之前就蒸发掉，所以雨并不能像人们直觉以为的那样灭火。关键原因是“到达之前就蒸发”，不是雨量太少或被风吹走。

optionRationalesZh.a: 文中没有说问题在于降水量不足，而是说雨在落到火上之前就蒸发了。
optionRationalesZh.b: 风在火风暴中确实存在，但这里讲者没有把“风把雨吹走”作为主要原因。
optionRationalesZh.c: 正确。这正是原文给出的直接解释。
optionRationalesZh.d: 文中提到的是带烟灰的雨，并未说烟灰会让火烧得更快。

public.prompt: What can be inferred if a firestorm reaches a place with no more vegetation to burn?

options.text: It will lose its fuel source and eventually die out
It will turn into an ordinary thunderstorm and keep raining
It will immediately produce more lightning than before
It will stop making clouds but keep spreading by wind alone

evidence.quote: A firestorm eventually ends when it runs out of a key ingredient: fuel for the fire below, killing the fire and taking the firestorm with it.

private.explanationZh: 讲者说火风暴最终会在下方火源失去“关键原料”时结束，这个原料就是可燃物。由此可推断，如果它到达一个没有更多植被可烧的地方，提供热量和湿气的基础就消失了，火风暴也会逐渐停止。

optionRationalesZh.a: 正确。这是对“燃料耗尽后火风暴结束”的直接推论。
optionRationalesZh.b: 文中没有说火风暴会自动转回普通雷暴。
optionRationalesZh.c: 缺少支持。可燃物耗尽通常意味着系统减弱，而不是闪电立刻增强。
optionRationalesZh.d: 风可能推动系统移动，但没有燃料时它不能仅靠风无限持续。

public.prompt: Why does the speaker mention Australia's Black Saturday bushfires?

options.text: To show that soot-blackened rain usually causes flooding
To compare Australian storms with those powered by the sun
To argue that most wildfires begin with long-distance winds
To give a real example of firestorms traveling and igniting many new fires

evidence.quote: air currents can shift and push a still-raging firestorm to a totally different region, where lightning can strike and start new fires - and firestorms.

private.explanationZh: 结尾提到黑色星期六，不是为了补充背景知识，而是为了把前面抽象说明的机制具体化：火风暴会被气流推到别处，再用闪电点燃新的火，从而形成连锁扩散。这个例子强化了火风暴危险性的现实意义。

optionRationalesZh.a: 文中没有借这个案例讨论洪水问题。
optionRationalesZh.b: 这里不是在比较澳大利亚天气和太阳加热形成的普通雷暴。
optionRationalesZh.c: 讲者强调的是火风暴移动后引发新火，不是说大多数野火都起于远距离风。
optionRationalesZh.d: 正确。这个真实案例正是用来展示火风暴如何传播并引燃更多火点。

reasonsZh.minute-earth-237-q01: 全文先用普通雷暴作对比，随后重点解释火风暴如何形成、为何能持续更久，以及如何被气流带到新地区并引发更多火灾，因此主旨是火风暴的形成、维持与扩散。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-237-q02: 原文明确说火的强烈热量通常会让雨在落到下面火场之前就蒸发，所以雨无法扑灭火。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-237-q03: 讲者说火风暴最终会在下方火势失去关键原料即燃料时结束；没有更多植被可烧，就会失去燃料来源并逐渐消亡。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-237-q04: 提到澳大利亚黑色星期六山火，是为了给前一句“火风暴可被气流推到别处、闪电引发新火灾”提供真实案例，说明火风暴会移动并点燃许多新火点。

public.prompt: What is the main topic of the talk?

options.text: How several cognitive biases can make a pandemic worse
Why COVID vaccines take years to distribute
How scientists track every infected person in real time
Why global charities focus on education during crises

evidence.quote: Here are four of those cognitive biases that have made the pandemic worse.

private.explanationZh: 讲者明确说接下来要介绍“四种”让疫情变得更糟的认知偏差。后文也围绕人类如何低估传播、过度乐观、偏向即时满足、以及反感被命令等心理倾向展开，所以主旨就是认知偏差如何加剧疫情。

optionRationalesZh.a: 正确。它准确概括了整段演讲的中心。
optionRationalesZh.b: 疫苗只是开头提到科学能力时顺带出现，不是主体。
optionRationalesZh.c: 全文没有讲实时追踪每一个感染者的方法。
optionRationalesZh.d: 教育和全球挑战出现在结尾相关材料介绍中，不是主线。

public.prompt: According to the talk, why do many people underestimate how quickly a disease can spread?

options.text: They assume most infected people recover within days
They naturally think of growth as linear rather than exponential
They expect weather to slow the spread automatically
They rely too heavily on doctors to make decisions

evidence.quote: We intuitively process "growth" as linear

private.explanationZh: 讲者指出，人们直觉上把“增长”理解成线性的，所以很难真正想象指数式扩散的速度。这种直觉错误会让人低估疾病传播的严重性，从而不愿采取严格预防措施。

optionRationalesZh.a: 文中讨论的是对传播速度的误判，不是对康复速度的假设。
optionRationalesZh.b: 正确。线性直觉和指数传播之间的落差正是讲者强调的关键。
optionRationalesZh.c: 原文没有把天气当作人们低估传播的主要原因。
optionRationalesZh.d: 文中批评的是认知偏差，而不是对医生的依赖。

public.prompt: Why does the speaker mention that people think they are above average and less likely than average to get in a car accident?

options.text: To show that intelligence tests are unreliable during crises
To suggest that careful drivers also follow health rules
To compare traffic safety campaigns with pandemic messaging
To illustrate the optimism bias that makes people downplay their own risk

evidence.quote: Evidence shows that we think we're above average as far as intelligence, and less likely than average to get in a car accident. So it's no surprise that many people underestimate their likelihood of getting COVID - and act accordingly.

private.explanationZh: 这里的两个例子都服务于同一个论点：人们常常把自己看得比平均水平更安全、更幸运，因此会低估自己感染 COVID 的可能性。讲者借这些熟悉的偏差说明“过度乐观”如何转化为危险行为。

optionRationalesZh.a: 讲者不是在批评智力测试本身，而是在说明人类会高估自己。
optionRationalesZh.b: 原文没有讨论开车谨慎与防疫行为之间的对应关系。
optionRationalesZh.c: 提到车祸概率不是为了比较宣传方式，而是为了举例说明乐观偏差。
optionRationalesZh.d: 正确。这个例子正是用来解释人们为何会低估自身风险。

public.prompt: How is the talk mainly organized?

options.text: It contrasts two diseases and then explains which one is harder to control
It presents the history of a pandemic and then predicts its exact ending
It describes several biases that worsen a pandemic and then shifts to ways to counter them
It lists medical technologies first and then argues that human behavior does not matter

evidence.quote: Here are four of those cognitive biases that have made the pandemic worse.

In addition to discussing how we can mitigate some of the worst fallout from this pandemic - through equitable vaccine distribution and a huge investment in getting stalled educational opportunities back on track - they also dig into how we can prevent the next pandemic.

private.explanationZh: 全文前半部分依次介绍让疫情恶化的多种认知偏差，后半部分再转向“如何对抗这些错误本能”以及如何预防下一次大流行。因此最准确的结构概括是“先讲问题，再讲应对与延伸”。

optionRationalesZh.a: 文中没有用“两种疾病对比”来组织内容。
optionRationalesZh.b: 全文也不是按历史时间线展开，更没有预测疫情的确切结局。
optionRationalesZh.c: 正确。这一选项最准确反映了演讲的整体结构。
optionRationalesZh.d: 医疗技术在结尾出现，但不是先列技术、再否定行为因素。

reasonsZh.minute-earth-238-q01: 开头直接说人脑的若干认知偏差让疫情更严重，后文依次举出四种偏差并说明其后果，所以主旨是这些偏差如何加剧大流行。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-238-q02: 原文说人类直觉上把“增长”按线性处理，而像COVID这样的感染会指数式传播，所以人们会低估扩散速度。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-238-q03: 讲者用“人们觉得自己高于平均水平、车祸概率低于平均”来举例说明人天生偏乐观，因此会低估自己感染COVID的风险。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-238-q04: 结构上先列出四种让疫情恶化的认知偏差，接着再转到如何对抗这些偏差，如定期信息报告和正向强化，因此选描述“先讲偏差再讲应对办法”的选项。

public.prompt: What is the main point of the talk?

options.text: Cats are genetically simple because they have been domesticated recently
Visible differences among animals usually match differences in total DNA
Humans can create new dog breeds only by increasing mutation rates
Dogs can look extremely different even though they are not very diverse genetically overall

evidence.quote: So why do dogs look and act so doggone distinctive, compared to humans or cats? Well, because it matters how genetic differences are distributed within a species.

private.explanationZh: 讲者整段都在解释一个看似矛盾的现象：狗在外表和行为上差异巨大，但总体遗传多样性却并不高。关键不在“总量多不多”，而在基因差异如何被分配到不同群体里。

optionRationalesZh.a: 讲者并没有把猫的情况简单归结为“最近才驯化，所以基因简单”。
optionRationalesZh.b: 这正是演讲要纠正的误解；外表差异不一定等于总体 DNA 差异更大。
optionRationalesZh.c: 文中强调的是选择和分配已有变异，不是提高突变率。
optionRationalesZh.d: 正确。它准确抓住了题目中的“多样性悖论”。

public.prompt: Why does the speaker use the example of colored dots divided into groups?

options.text: To show that the distribution of differences within groups can matter more than the total amount of difference
To demonstrate that cats have four main genetic lineages
To explain how scientists color-code genes in a laboratory
To prove that every dog breed contains exactly one defining trait

evidence.quote: Instead of genetic differences, let's look at colored dots.

private.explanationZh: 彩色点并不是在介绍真实基因，而是一个类比工具。讲者用它帮助听众理解：即使总体上有同样多的差异，如果这些差异被集中到不同组之间，就会产生“组内很像、组间很不一样”的效果，这正对应狗的品种分化。

optionRationalesZh.a: 正确。这个类比的核心就是说明“差异如何分布”比“差异总量有多少”更关键。
optionRationalesZh.b: 四种颜色只是示意，不是在讲猫的四条真实谱系。
optionRationalesZh.c: 这里不是实验方法介绍，而是概念说明。
optionRationalesZh.d: 类比没有说每个品种只有一个特征，更没有证明“恰好一个”。

public.prompt: According to the talk, how have humans made dogs from different breeds so distinct from one another?

options.text: By crossbreeding dogs freely with wild canines
By increasing the total number of genes in dogs
By concentrating different gene variants into separate breeding groups over many generations
By training puppies to develop different physical traits after birth

evidence.quote: Over centuries of careful breeding, we've partitioned different genes and variants of genes into different groups of dogs.

private.explanationZh: 讲者明确说，经过数百年的精心繁育，人类把不同基因和基因变体“分区”到了不同的狗群体中。这样一来，同一品种内部更相似，不同品种之间更不同，于是少量关键差异就能造成显著外观差别。

optionRationalesZh.a: 原文没有说通过与野生犬类自由杂交来制造差异。
optionRationalesZh.b: 讲者讨论的是基因变体的分配，而不是基因总数增加。
optionRationalesZh.c: 正确。这正是文中给出的机制说明。
optionRationalesZh.d: 训练会影响行为，但不能解释文中强调的遗传性外形差异。

public.prompt: What does the speaker suggest could happen if people carefully selected cats for another hundred years?

options.text: Cats would lose most of their existing genetic diversity
Cats could probably come to vary as much as dogs do
Cats would become genetically more uniform than chickens
Cats would stop showing any meaningful breed differences

evidence.quote: given another hundred years of careful selection, we could probably breed cats that vary as much as dogs.

private.explanationZh: 结尾处讲者直接提出一个推测：如果再经过一百年的精细选择，猫也“很可能”被培育得像狗一样多样。这是基于前文关于人为选择与差异分配的讨论得出的预测。

optionRationalesZh.a: 文中没有说猫会失去大部分现有遗传多样性。
optionRationalesZh.b: 正确。这正是讲者在结尾明确提出的预测。
optionRationalesZh.c: 原文提到鸡只是说明狗并非唯一特例，并未把猫和鸡作这种比较。
optionRationalesZh.d: 这与讲者的意思相反；他是在说猫的差异可能会更大。

reasonsZh.minute-earth-239-q01: 全文核心矛盾是狗外表和行为上差异极大，但整体DNA差异其实不大；后文都在解释为何会出现这种现象。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-239-q02: 彩色点分组的例子是为了说明，关键不只是总共有多少差异，还在于这些差异如何分布在各组内部与各组之间，这正对应狗品种之间的情况。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-239-q03: 原文说经过数百年精心繁育，人类把不同基因及其变体分配到不同狗群体中，因此不同品种彼此很不一样。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-239-q04: 讲者明确说，如果再经过一百年的精心选择繁育，猫“很可能也会像狗一样差异那么大”。

public.prompt: What is the talk mainly trying to do?

options.text: Prove that all artistic images of viruses are misleading
Describe the history of coronavirus research from the first outbreak to the present
Explain how coronavirus enters cells so different visual depictions can be evaluated
Argue that watercolor paintings are the best way to communicate molecular science

evidence.quote: I really wanted, not only to read this David Goodsell painting, but also understand what's going on with all of these other representations.
An impressive process that will surely keep my mind curious for a long time to come, but for now, this is all we need to know to review the various depictions at the beginning of the video.

private.explanationZh: 讲者一开始就说自己想读懂这幅画以及其他表现方式，随后专门讲解病毒入侵细胞的基础过程，并明确表示这些知识“就是为了回头审视前面那些图像”。因此主旨是借解释感染机制来评估不同描绘。

optionRationalesZh.a: 讲者并没有一概否定所有艺术图像，而是在比较它们的准确程度。
optionRationalesZh.b: 全文不是按研究史展开。
optionRationalesZh.c: 正确。这个选项同时涵盖了解释机制和评估图像两层目的。
optionRationalesZh.d: 他高度评价这幅水彩画，但全文目的不是论证“水彩是最佳媒介”。

public.prompt: According to the talk, what happens after other cell structures cut the spike protein in specific places?

options.text: A hidden structure shoots into the cell membrane and helps pull the two membranes together
The virus immediately copies its genetic material outside the cell
The ACE2 receptor breaks apart and releases energy into the cell
The cell membrane hardens so the virus cannot leave later

evidence.quote: some other cell structures are tricked into cutting the spike protein in very specific locations, which releases a spring loaded harpoon that will pierce into the cell's membrane and then coil into itself, pulling the membranes of the cell and the virus close enough to fuse.

private.explanationZh: 讲者详细描述了这一连串动作：刺突蛋白被特定位点切开后，会释放出像弹簧鱼叉一样的结构，刺入细胞膜，再卷曲，把病毒膜和细胞膜拉近到足以融合。这个步骤直接解释了病毒遗传物质如何进入细胞。

optionRationalesZh.a: 正确。这一选项准确概括了原文描述的“鱼叉”和膜融合过程。
optionRationalesZh.b: 复制发生在进入细胞之后，不是在这一步、也不是在细胞外。
optionRationalesZh.c: 原文没有说 ACE2 受体在这里碎裂并释放能量。
optionRationalesZh.d: 讲者讲的是膜融合，不是膜变硬。

public.prompt: Why does the speaker compare viruses to ink droplets and mention that virologists dislike anthropomorphizing them?

options.text: To argue that viruses should be classified as a kind of cell
To emphasize that viruses outside cells are inert particles moved by diffusion, not intention
To show that diffusion is less important than active movement during infection
To explain why artistic depictions should never simplify scientific ideas

evidence.quote: Even after entering an organism, viruses outside a cell are just inert particles; and the only reason they get from one place to another is because there are *millions* of them being bounced around thanks to the power of diffusion.

private.explanationZh: 这里的比喻和补充说明都在服务同一个观念：病毒在细胞外并不是有意图地“追逐”目标，而是像墨滴一样被扩散推动。讲者提到拟人化问题，是为了提醒听众不要把病毒误解成主动行动的生物体。

optionRationalesZh.a: 原文没有主张把病毒归类为细胞，反而强调它与细胞不同。
optionRationalesZh.b: 正确。这个选项准确抓住了比喻的解释功能。
optionRationalesZh.c: 讲者没有贬低扩散的重要性，恰恰相反，他在强调扩散。
optionRationalesZh.d: 他承认自己会做艺术化处理，但这里不是在禁止简化，而是在澄清科学含义。

public.prompt: What can be inferred about David Goodsell's painting from the speaker's comments?

options.text: It is visually impressive but mostly unsupported by current science
It becomes accurate only if the missing fishing rods are added later
It is less useful than simpler diagrams for explaining cell entry
It is highly informative but still includes some informed guesses because some details remain unknown

evidence.quote: this painting has some degree of informed speculation, since we don't know everything about the coronavirus infection cycle.

private.explanationZh: 讲者总体上高度评价这幅画的科学准确性，但同时指出它也包含“有根据的推测”，因为关于病毒进入过程的若干细节目前仍未完全确定。所以最合理的推断是：这幅画非常有价值，但并非每一处都已经被完全证实。

optionRationalesZh.a: 这与讲者的总体评价不符；他认为这幅画相当出色而不是“大多没有科学依据”。
optionRationalesZh.b: 缺失 fishing rods 只是一个局部备注，不代表加上它们就能解决所有不确定性。
optionRationalesZh.c: 讲者最后把它当作最出色的表现之一，并没有说它不如简单图示。
optionRationalesZh.d: 正确。这一选项最完整反映了“高度准确但仍含推测”的双重评价。

reasonsZh.minute-earth-240-q01: 讲者先表示想读懂并评估不同冠状病毒图像，随后系统解释病毒如何附着并进入细胞，最后据此评论各种描绘，所以主要目的是解释入侵过程以便评价图像表现。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-240-q02: 原文说切开刺突蛋白后会释放一个“弹簧加载的鱼叉”，刺入细胞膜并卷曲，把病毒膜和细胞膜拉近到足以融合。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-240-q03: 把病毒比作墨滴、并提到病毒学家不喜欢把病毒拟人化，是为了强调细胞外的病毒不是有意图的活物，而是被扩散推动的惰性颗粒。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-240-q04: 讲者高度评价这幅画展示了多种关键结构和过程，但也明确说其中有一定“有根据的推测”，因为仍有一些感染细节未知，所以应选“信息量高但仍含推测”的选项。

public.prompt: What is the main argument of the talk?

options.text: Maximum life expectancy is the only fair way to compare species
A single life expectancy number can be misleading, so survival curves give a better overall picture
Dogs and humans should be compared only after infancy
Sea turtles are unusual because they do not experience infant mortality

evidence.quote: Instead of focusing on a singular number, we can actually learn much more about animals by graphing all the different ages when they die which we can convert into an equivalent graph, showing what percentage of individuals are expected to survive to any given age.
but instead to look at the big picture and take a curve's eye view.

private.explanationZh: 讲者先指出不同物种常被用不同定义计算“预期寿命”，所以单一数字难以公平比较；随后提出更好的办法是看生存曲线，从整体角度理解生命历程。因此中心论点是“不要只盯一个寿命数字，要看更完整的曲线图景”。

optionRationalesZh.a: 讲者明确反对只看 maximum life expectancy。
optionRationalesZh.b: 正确。它完整概括了问题与解决思路。
optionRationalesZh.c: 这只是对狗的一种常用算法说明，不是全文主张。
optionRationalesZh.d: 文中恰好说海龟幼体死亡率很高，所以这个说法错误。

public.prompt: Why does the talk say dog life expectancy is often calculated after babyhood?

options.text: Because dogs are born at very different times of year
Because scientists do not have enough data on puppies
Because dogs rarely die before adulthood
Because owners usually want to know how long dogs are expected to live once they have survived infancy

evidence.quote: likely because pet owners mostly care about how long their dogs lives are expected to be, given that they've already survived infancy.

private.explanationZh: 讲者解释说，狗通常采用“过了婴儿期之后的预期寿命”，很可能是因为宠物主人更关心已经活过幼年期的狗还能活多久。这是一个使用场景上的关注点，而不是数据缺失或幼犬几乎不死。

optionRationalesZh.a: 出生季节差异并不是这里给出的原因。
optionRationalesZh.b: 原文没有说是因为幼犬数据不足。
optionRationalesZh.c: 文中没有说狗很少在成年前死亡，相反还特地把早死个体排除在算法外。
optionRationalesZh.d: 正确。这正是讲者给出的解释。

public.prompt: Why does the speaker discuss the drop between the first and second bars of a survival curve?

options.text: To explain how the curve can reveal infant mortality and the amount of parental care
To show that dogs and humans have identical early-life risks
To argue that graphs are more reliable than direct observation
To prove that sea turtles are always healthier than mammals

evidence.quote: For example, this dropoff between the first and second bars represents the infant mortality rate.

private.explanationZh: 讲者提到前两根柱子的下降，是为了教听众“怎么读这种曲线”。这个位置反映的是婴儿期死亡率，而不同物种在这里下降多少，又能提示父母照料程度等生命史差异，所以它的作用是解释图表意义。

optionRationalesZh.a: 正确。这个部分就是在说明曲线早期下降所代表的生物学信息。
optionRationalesZh.b: 文中没有说人类和狗的早期风险相同，只是说二者都比海龟下降小。
optionRationalesZh.c: 讲者是在解释图表含义，不是在抽象争论图表与观察谁更可靠。
optionRationalesZh.d: 这也不是为了证明海龟“总是更健康”；海龟早期反而更危险。

public.prompt: What can be inferred when a species' survival curve becomes flatter near the end, as in the sea turtle example?

options.text: Most individuals die at exactly the same old age
The species has a lower infant mortality rate than humans
The few individuals that reach old age may face less risk than they did earlier in life
The species' maximum life expectancy must be shorter than average

evidence.quote: But the distribution curve of some animals, like sea turtles, instead gets flatter, suggesting that the few individuals that make it to super seniorhood not only don't get sick, but are actually better protected from death than when they were younger.

private.explanationZh: 讲者说海龟这类动物在曲线末端不是像人和狗那样“突然掉下去”，而是变得更平，说明那些活到高龄的少数个体可能比年轻时更不容易死亡。这个推论关注的是老年阶段的相对风险变化，而不是所有个体死在同一年龄。

optionRationalesZh.a: 曲线变平不等于大家在同一个年龄死亡。
optionRationalesZh.b: 这是末端趋势，不能推出婴儿死亡率更低；前文其实说海龟幼体死亡率很高。
optionRationalesZh.c: 正确。这正是讲者对这种更平缓末端给出的解释。
optionRationalesZh.d: 曲线末端变平并不能推出最大寿命更短。

reasonsZh.minute-earth-241-q01: 讲者开头就指出不同物种的“life expectancy”常按不同定义计算，因此单一数字难以比较；随后主张用生存曲线看整体图景更有意义。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-241-q02: 原文明确说明，对狗常用“婴儿期之后的预期寿命”，因为宠物主人更关心狗在已经活过幼年后还能活多久。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-241-q03: 讲第一、第二根柱子之间的下降，是为了说明那代表婴儿死亡率，并进一步反映父母照料程度高低。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-241-q04: 讲者说像海龟那样在末端变平，意味着少数活到超级高龄的个体“不仅不更容易生病，反而比年轻时更受保护、不易死亡”，因此可推断它们老年阶段风险更低。

public.prompt: What is the main point of the talk?

options.text: Scientists should avoid testing ideas that seem likely to fail.
Null results can still advance science by ruling out possibilities and adding knowledge.
Dark matter research matters more than medical research.
Researchers usually publish only exciting positive findings.

evidence.quote: finding "nothing" - or no effect - is still finding "something," because you have knowledge you didn't have before.

private.explanationZh: 本题考查主旨。开头明确说“发现没有效果”仍然是在获得新知识，整段随后用卧床休息和暗物质两个例子展开，核心都在说明零结果虽然没有验证原假设，却能排除错误方向、增加理解，因此对科学推进仍然有价值。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。讲话者并没有主张应避免可能失败的测试，反而强调即使假设不成立，研究也有价值。
optionRationalesZh.b: 正确。这个选项准确概括了“零结果仍能提供知识、缩小可能范围”的中心论点。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。暗物质和医学都只是例子，讲话者没有比较二者谁更重要。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。文中没有讨论发表偏好，重点是零结果本身的科学意义。

public.prompt: According to the talk, how do null results help scientists searching for dark matter?

options.text: They prove that dark matter does not exist.
They show that dark matter must be visible.
They let scientists eliminate some particle sizes and refine their search.
They reveal exactly where dark matter is located.

evidence.quote: each time they fail to detect anything, scientists can eliminate that size as a possibility, adjust their models and equipment, and narrow the scope of their search.

private.explanationZh: 本题是细节题。原文直接说明，探测器没有发现目标时，科学家就可以排除某一类粒子大小，并据此调整模型和设备，所以零结果是在帮助他们把搜索范围越缩越小。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。没有探测到并不等于证明不存在，原文说的是排除部分可能性。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。文中强调“dark”指难以探测，不是说它必须可见。
optionRationalesZh.c: 正确。这正是原文对零结果作用的直接概括。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。零结果不能直接告诉科学家暗物质具体在哪里。

public.prompt: Why does the speaker compare null results to looking for a lost phone?

options.text: To show that failing to find something can still narrow the search.
To suggest that dark matter is probably inside ordinary devices.
To argue that everyday problems are harder than scientific ones.
To explain why scientists should stop using complicated equipment.

evidence.quote: It's kind of like looking for a lost phone - even if you don't find it where you look, you learn where it isn't, letting you focus your efforts elsewhere.

private.explanationZh: 本题考查举例目的。丢手机的类比不是在谈手机本身，而是在把抽象的科研逻辑具体化：即使没找到目标，也知道哪些地方不是答案，从而把后续努力集中到更可能的方向。

optionRationalesZh.a: 正确。这个类比正是为了说明“没找到”也能缩小搜索范围。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。类比没有暗示暗物质藏在设备里，这属于无中生有。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。讲话者不是比较哪种问题更难，而是在说明思维过程。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。文中并未借此反对复杂设备，反而说科学家会据零结果调整设备。

public.prompt: What can be inferred about the speaker's attitude toward being wrong in science?

options.text: It is embarrassing but unavoidable in public communication.
It is useful only when the results concern major discoveries.
It shows that a hypothesis was poorly designed from the start.
It is a productive part of learning rather than a true failure.

evidence.quote: But being wrong is far from failing - after all, you're still learning something new, and science is all about increasing our understanding of the world around us.

private.explanationZh: 本题是推断题。结尾明确把“错误”与“失败”区分开来，讲话者认为只要研究让人获得新认识，它就是有意义的。因此可以推断，他把出错看作科学学习过程中的正常且有产出的部分。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。文中没有把重点放在公开表达时的尴尬感上。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。前面还举了“大头不会增加点赞”这类小例子，说明并非只有重大研究才有价值。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。假设被证伪不等于设计一定糟糕，科学本来就要测试可能出错的想法。
optionRationalesZh.d: 正确。这一选项最符合结尾对“being wrong”的正面定位。

reasonsZh.minute-earth-242-q01: 讲座开头先定义“null result”，随后用卧床保胎和暗物质两个例子说明：即使没有发现预期效果，研究仍能提供新知识、排除错误方向，并帮助缩小后续搜索范围，所以主旨是空结果也能推进科学。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-242-q02: 原文明确说某些探测器按特定粒径校准，每次“什么也没探测到”，科学家就能排除那种粒径，并调整模型和设备、缩小搜索范围，因此答案是排除部分粒径并改进搜索。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-242-q03: “找丢失的手机”这个类比紧接在暗物质例子后面，作用是说明即便没找到目标，也知道了它不在什么地方，从而把精力集中到别处，即“没找到也能缩小搜索范围”。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-242-q04: 说话者承认“being wrong can feel like failing”，但后文反复强调“still learning something new”“being wrong is far from failing”，可推断他认为在科学中出错是学习过程的一部分，而不是真正的失败。

public.prompt: How does the speaker organize the talk?

options.text: By describing a medical mystery and then arguing that it has no answer.
By listing famous survival cases before comparing different animal species.
By contrasting the body's upper heat limit with its lower cold limit to explain the difference.
By explaining how body temperature is measured and then criticizing that method.

evidence.quote: the highest body temp we know someone survived isn't that far above 'normal', but the lowest is more than twice as far in the other direction.
There's way more wiggle room in the other direction.

private.explanationZh: 本题考查篇章结构。开头先提出“为什么人能承受更低温却不能承受高太多的温度”这个对比性问题，随后先讲高温怎样破坏蛋白质和细胞成分，再转向低温主要是让反应变慢，所以整段是通过冷热对照来解释两边的差异。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。讲话者并没有说这个问题无解，整段都在给出生物学解释。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。文中没有先罗列多个生还案例，也没有把重点组织成动物物种比较。
optionRationalesZh.c: 正确。这一项准确概括了从高温到低温的对照式组织方式。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。体温测量方法只是开头轻微提到，并不是全文组织主线。

public.prompt: According to the talk, what happens to some proteins when body temperature rises above about 40 degrees Celsius?

options.text: They become more efficient permanently.
They begin to uncoil and form clumps.
They stop interacting with membranes but keep their shape.
They turn into extra energy for the immune system.

evidence.quote: Over 40 degrees or so, certain proteins get so loose that they start to uncoil completely.

private.explanationZh: 本题是细节题。原文说超过大约40度时，一些蛋白质会松到完全解开，后面又补充这些解开的蛋白质会缠成团块，所以正确内容是“解开并结团”。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。原文说的是过热导致结构崩坏，不是永久提升效率。
optionRationalesZh.b: 正确。它完整对应了“uncoil completely”和后文“clumps”的信息。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。文中说细胞膜等也会出问题，但不是“蛋白质保持形状不变”。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。原文没有提到蛋白质转化成免疫系统额外能量。

public.prompt: Why does the speaker mention chilled organs and induced hypothermia?

options.text: To show that doctors prefer cold treatments to all other medical treatments.
To argue that bears are the best model for human medicine.
To explain that cold completely stops biological activity.
To illustrate that slowing reactions can sometimes protect tissues instead of harming them.

evidence.quote: This slow-down isn't inherently deadly - it can actually be helpful, because the slower reactions happen, the less energy and oxygen organs require.

private.explanationZh: 本题考查举例目的。讲话者提到器官移植前降温和脑损伤后的诱导性低温，是为了证明“变冷导致反应变慢”不一定是坏事；在某些情况下，需求下降反而能帮助器官和组织撑过去。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。举例不是为了宣称冷疗优于所有其他治疗。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。熊只是后面另一个例子，不是这里提器官降温的核心目的。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。原文强调的是反应变慢，而不是完全停止。
optionRationalesZh.d: 正确。这正是两个医疗例子所服务的论点。

public.prompt: What can be inferred about the effects of extreme cold on the human body?

options.text: They are often reversible if organs keep functioning and the body is rewarmed carefully.
They damage proteins more suddenly than extreme heat does.
They help the body operate closer to its normal peak efficiency.
They are harmless as long as a person stays conscious.

evidence.quote: as long as the temperature of a person - or a snoozing bear - stays just high enough to keep our organs working, we can generally reverse the problems caused by cold...just warm up again (slowly!) and things will go more or less back to normal.

private.explanationZh: 本题是推断题。原文没有说低温完全无害，而是说只要器官还能维持运作，之后再缓慢复温，很多由寒冷引起的问题通常可以逆转。因此可推断低温伤害往往具有可恢复性，但前提条件很关键。

optionRationalesZh.a: 正确。它准确整合了“器官仍在工作”和“慢慢升温后大体恢复正常”两层条件。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。文中相反强调高温更容易让关键分子直接损坏。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。低温使反应变慢，不是让身体更接近高效状态。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。是否清醒并不是文中的判断标准，而且低温绝非“无害”。

reasonsZh.minute-earth-243-q01: 全篇先解释人体为什么离高温极限很近：蛋白质、膜和通道在40到47摄氏度附近会失效；随后转到低温，说明变冷主要是让反应减慢，因此身体在低温方向有更大缓冲空间。结构上就是对比上限和下限来回答开头问题。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-243-q02: 原文直接说超过40摄氏度左右，某些蛋白质会“uncoil completely”，随后这些展开的蛋白质会“start tangling up in tight clumps”，与选项b完全对应。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-243-q03: 提到冷藏器官和诱导低温，不是为了夸大低温疗法，而是为了举例说明：反应变慢本身不一定致命，反而可能减少器官对能量和氧气的需求，从而起到保护作用。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-243-q04: 结尾说明只要体温仍足以维持器官工作，就“can generally reverse the problems caused by cold”，只需“warm up again (slowly!)”，因此可推断极端低温造成的问题往往在器官未停摆且复温得当时具有可逆性。

public.prompt: What is the main purpose of the talk?

options.text: To explain what proteins are and how their structures allow many biological functions.
To compare proteins with vitamins and show why vitamins are more versatile.
To argue that molecular diagrams are too confusing to be useful.
To describe how one laboratory first discovered amino acids.

evidence.quote: before explaining what proteins really are, and what they're made of
the incredible diversity of protein structures are what make it possible for proteins to play central roles in almost every biological process

private.explanationZh: 本题考查主旨。讲话者先说自己要解释蛋白质究竟是什么、由什么构成，随后说明不同表示法、氨基酸链如何折叠，以及这种结构多样性为何让蛋白质承担几乎所有生物过程中的关键功能，所以全文核心是“结构与功能”。

optionRationalesZh.a: 正确。它同时覆盖了蛋白质的构成、折叠结构和功能意义。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。文中没有把蛋白质与维生素作主线比较。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。虽然提到多种分子表示法，但不是要否定它们，而是解释它们为什么有用。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。讲话者没有讲某个实验室发现氨基酸的历史过程。

public.prompt: Why are side chains omitted from a ribbon diagram?

options.text: They are too small to contain useful information.
They change too quickly to be represented accurately.
They appear only in plant proteins.
They would hide the overall folded structure.

evidence.quote: Side chains are attached to the backbone, but they aren't displayed in the ribbon diagram because that would obscure the structure.

private.explanationZh: 本题是细节题。原文直接说侧链没有画出来，因为如果画出来就会把整体结构遮住。也就是说，省略侧链是为了让听众更清楚地看到蛋白质主链如何折叠。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。文中没有说侧链太小、没有信息。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。侧链被省略的原因不是“变化太快”，而是显示后会遮挡结构。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。氨基酸侧链是普遍存在的，不是植物蛋白特有。
optionRationalesZh.d: 正确。这与原文中的“obscure the structure”完全对应。

public.prompt: Why does the speaker compare amino-acid interactions to magnets?

options.text: To argue that proteins are made of metal-like materials.
To help listeners picture how attraction and repulsion make a chain fold.
To suggest that protein folding depends mainly on electricity from outside the cell.
To show that helical shapes are stronger than flat sheets.

evidence.quote: allowing them to interact by attraction and repulsion, just like magnets.

private.explanationZh: 本题考查类比作用。磁铁是听众熟悉的经验对象，讲话者借它来帮助理解分子世界里“吸引与排斥”如何让一条氨基酸链不断折叠，最终形成稳定构型。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。类比并不是说蛋白质本身像金属一样构成。
optionRationalesZh.b: 正确。它准确说明了磁铁类比服务于解释折叠机制。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。原文没有把重点放在外部电流或细胞外电力上。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。讲话者不是用磁铁比较螺旋和片层谁更结实。

public.prompt: What can be inferred from the discussion of eating one organism and building proteins in another?

options.text: Most dietary proteins stay intact as they move between organisms.
Plants do not need proteins because they make energy from sunlight.
Organisms reuse amino acids from food to assemble new proteins for their own needs.
Only muscle proteins can be rebuilt after digestion.

evidence.quote: the proteins in the unlucky one will be split back into amino acids, which will then be recycled to create all sorts of new proteins for the lucky organism

private.explanationZh: 本题是推断题。原文说吃进去的蛋白质会先被拆成氨基酸，然后这些氨基酸再被“回收”去制造新的蛋白质。由此可推断，生物不是把原来的蛋白质整件搬运，而是重组原料来满足自身需要。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。文中明确说蛋白质会先被分解，不会原封不动转移。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。讲话者专门说植物里也充满蛋白质。
optionRationalesZh.c: 正确。它准确概括了“分解后再利用氨基酸组装新蛋白”的过程。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。被重建的不只是肌肉蛋白，而是“all sorts of new proteins”。

reasonsZh.minute-earth-244-q01: 讲座主体依次解释蛋白质是什么、为什么需要不同表示方式、氨基酸如何组成并折叠成蛋白质，以及这些结构差异为何让蛋白质承担众多生物功能，所以主旨是说明蛋白质及其结构与功能的关系。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-244-q02: 原文明确说侧链没有显示在ribbon diagram里，因为“that would obscure the structure”，也就是会遮住整体折叠结构，因此选d。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-244-q03: 把氨基酸之间的作用比作磁铁，是为了帮助听者直观理解吸引与排斥如何使一条链折成不同形状，不是在说蛋白质像金属，也不是讨论外部电力。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-244-q04: 最后一部分说一个生物吃掉另一个后，蛋白质会先被分解回氨基酸，再被“recycled to create all sorts of new proteins”，可推断生物会把食物中的氨基酸重新利用，合成自己需要的新蛋白质。

public.prompt: What central idea about carbon sequestration does the superhero comparison introduce?

options.text: Trees are the only organisms that matter for long-term carbon storage.
Forest fires always reduce biodiversity and productivity.
Carbon sequestration depends mostly on how quickly one tree species can grow.
Diverse species and even whole ecosystems can work together to increase carbon sequestration.

evidence.quote: their diverse assortment of super-powers is key to their collective success.
A diverse forest can sequester twice as much carbon - or more - as a single species stand.

private.explanationZh: 本题考查主旨。全文借超级英雄团队作比，说明碳封存不是靠单一“明星物种”，而是依赖树种多样性、非树种“配角”，甚至更大尺度上的生态系统协同。主线一直在强调“多样合作带来更强的封存能力”。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。讲话者明确提到真菌、动物、微生物乃至整个生态系统的重要作用。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。文中谈到控制性燃烧可能提升多样性和生产力，不是“一定降低”。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。单一树种恰恰因为策略相同、彼此干扰而不利于封存。
optionRationalesZh.d: 正确。这一项最完整地概括了从树种多样性扩展到生态系统协作的中心思想。

public.prompt: Why does the speaker say a stand of just one tree species stores less carbon?

options.text: Because the trees tend to compete using the same strategy.
Because a single species cannot produce leaves and roots at the same time.
Because microbes remove carbon faster in simple forests.
Because only tropical forests can contain enough biomass.

evidence.quote: In a stand of just one species, everyone uses the same strategy, which means they quickly start interfering with each other, and nobody grows very big very fast; as a result, they can't sequester much carbon.

private.explanationZh: 本题是细节题。原文直接指出单一树种林分里，所有树都在用相同策略争夺资源，于是很快相互干扰，长得不够大也不够快，最终导致碳封存量较低。

optionRationalesZh.a: 正确。它完整复述了“同策略竞争导致互相干扰”的因果链。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。讲话者没有说单一树种不能同时长叶和长根。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。这里解释的是树与树之间策略重叠，不是微生物加速移除碳。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。文中没有把原因归结为只有热带森林才有足够生物量。

public.prompt: Why does the speaker mention fungi, animals, and microbes after discussing trees?

options.text: To show that most carbon is stored outside forests.
To explain that carbon sequestration depends only on decomposition.
To emphasize that species storing little carbon themselves can still strongly support the ecosystem's total storage.
To argue that trees are less important than mammals in every ecosystem.

evidence.quote: while lots of non-tree species don't store much carbon on their own, they can have outsized effects on their ecosystem's total haul.

private.explanationZh: 本题考查举例目的。讲话者先说树是主角，再引入真菌、动物和微生物这些“配角”，是为了补充一个关键观点：某些物种本身储碳不多，但它们通过帮助吸收资源、搬运种子和形成土壤，能显著提高整个系统的总封存量。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。文中没有说大部分碳都储存在森林之外。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。分解只是微生物的一个作用，绝不是“唯一依赖”。
optionRationalesZh.c: 正确。它准确抓住了这些非树种例子的论证功能。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。讲话者并未贬低树的重要性，只是在扩展团队概念。

public.prompt: What broader idea is introduced near the end of the talk?

options.text: Carbon sequestration can be predicted only from tree height.
Different ecosystems may reinforce one another's productivity across large regions.
Indigenous burning always replaces the need for biodiversity.
Scientists have already measured every ecosystem interaction precisely.

evidence.quote: entire ecosystems, from grasslands to tundra to oceans - may work together to boost productivity across entire regions, enabling our planet to sequester carbon over giant scales.

private.explanationZh: 本题是推断题。结尾把论证从“森林内部多样性”进一步扩大到“不同生态系统之间也可能协同”，说明讲话者在引入一个更宏观的观点：区域尺度上的生产力和碳封存也许来自跨生态系统的共同作用。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。树高不是结尾提出的宏观思想。
optionRationalesZh.b: 正确。它准确概括了结尾从森林扩展到草原、苔原和海洋的区域协同设想。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。控制性燃烧只是一个人类管理例子，不能替代多样性本身。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。原文明确说这类影响“we're still working to measure”，并未完全测清。

reasonsZh.minute-earth-245-q01: 超级英雄类比贯穿全篇：先说不同树种协同能储存更多碳，再扩展到真菌、动物、微生物和人类管理作用，最后进一步提出整个生态系统之间也可能协同提升生产力，所以中心思想是多样物种乃至多个生态系统共同促进碳汇。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-245-q02: 讲座明确说单一树种林分里“everyone uses the same strategy”，因此会更快彼此干扰、长得不够大不够快，结果无法储存太多碳，所以原因就是它们用同样策略相互竞争。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-245-q03: 在树木之后提到真菌、动物和微生物，是为了说明虽然很多非树种自己储碳不多，但它们能通过帮助吸收资源、传播种子和养分、形成土壤等方式，对整个生态系统的总碳储量产生很大影响。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-245-q04: 末尾引入的新层次是：不仅物种之间能协同，连草原、苔原、海洋等“entire ecosystems”也可能“work together to boost productivity across entire regions”，因此答案是不同生态系统可能在大范围内相互增强生产力。

public.prompt: What is the talk mainly about?

options.text: Why ocean waves are becoming larger because of climate change.
How underwater seafloor shapes can concentrate wave energy and create extremely large breaks.
Why surfers prefer waves near river mouths instead of reefs.
How sponsor-built globes use magnetism to model the oceans.

evidence.quote: The biggest waves on Earth - those that are the height of entire office buildings - become truly gigantic because of what happens out of sight.
the seafloor doesn't always slope gently, and its shape is what makes or breaks a break.

private.explanationZh: 本题考查主旨。讲话者从一开始就指出巨浪之所以巨大，是因为水面以下发生的事；随后全文围绕海底地形如何压缩、汇聚并抬升波浪能量展开，因此主旨是解释海底形状如何制造超大浪。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。文中没有把巨浪原因归结为气候变化。
optionRationalesZh.b: 正确。这一项准确概括了“海底地形集中波能并形成巨大破浪”的核心内容。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。讲话者不是在讨论冲浪者偏好，而是在解释物理机制。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。赞助商地球仪出现在后面，与主要科学内容无关。

public.prompt: According to the talk, what usually makes an ordinary wave break near shore?

options.text: Strong winds suddenly lift the water straight upward.
Deep water prevents the wave from slowing down.
A gently rising seafloor and friction make the wave unstable.
Cold ocean water causes the surface to harden.

evidence.quote: In most places around the world, the seafloor gently slopes upward, slowly pushing the wave's energy above the surface, while friction against the seafloor slows it down.

private.explanationZh: 本题是细节题。原文说明在大多数地方，海底会缓慢向上抬升，把波浪能量推向水面，同时海底摩擦让波浪减速，这两者共同造成不稳定水柱，最终破裂成浪。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。文中没有说普通破浪主要由强风突然托起造成。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。这里说的是浅水和摩擦导致破浪，不是深水阻止减速。
optionRationalesZh.c: 正确。它准确整合了“上升的海底”和“摩擦减速”两个关键条件。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。海水变冷导致表面硬化并非文中解释。

public.prompt: Why does the speaker compare bent wave energy to multiple onramps adding cars to a highway?

options.text: To illustrate how extra wave energy is redirected into the canyon and intensifies the wave.
To show that ocean waves and road traffic obey the same physical laws in every detail.
To argue that highways should be built farther from cliffs and beaches.
To explain why surfers can easily predict every large wave.

evidence.quote: Meanwhile, wave energy moving alongside the canyon's edges gets bent inside it too - like multiple onramps adding cars to a highway - further boosting the wave's energy.

private.explanationZh: 本题考查类比作用。这里的高速公路匝道类比是为了让听众直观看到：除了峡谷中本来的波能，旁边的波能也会被“并入车道”，一起进入狭窄通道，从而进一步增强浪的总能量。

optionRationalesZh.a: 正确。它准确说明了类比在解释“额外能量并入并增强波浪”这一点。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。类比只是帮助理解，不是说两套系统在每个细节上都完全一样。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。讲话者没有借此讨论公路规划。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。文中没有说因此就能轻易预测每一个巨浪。

public.prompt: What can be inferred about the Nazare Canyon's effect on waves?

options.text: It blocks wave energy so completely that waves vanish before shore.
It mainly creates waves by warming the water above the canyon.
It spreads wave energy across a wide area before the coast.
It keeps wave energy concentrated and then helps launch it upward near shore.

evidence.quote: That energy then gets launched up; the resulting ten-story-tall breaks are truly the biggest waves you will ever "sea."

private.explanationZh: 本题是推断题。结合前文峡谷不断收束能量、深到不足以提前触底减速，以及最后近乎垂直的海底把能量猛地向上抛起，可以推断它的关键作用是先保持并集中波能，再在靠岸处把能量发射上去。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。原文说的是形成世界上最大的破浪，不是让浪消失。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。讲话者没有把成因归结为海水升温。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。峡谷的作用恰恰是把能量集中到狭窄通道，而不是分散开。
optionRationalesZh.d: 正确。它最符合全文对Nazare峡谷作用机制的综合描述。

reasonsZh.minute-earth-246-q01: 讲座重点解释海底地形如何影响浪能：普通缓坡如何形成破浪，礁石和海底峡谷又如何压缩、汇聚并最终把能量向上推出，形成超大浪，因此主旨是海底地形集中浪能并制造巨大破浪。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-246-q02: 原文说多数地方海底会缓慢上升，把浪的能量慢慢推到水面以上，同时海底摩擦让波变慢，这会形成不稳定的水柱并最终崩落成破浪，所以选c。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-246-q03: “多个匝道汇入高速公路”的类比出现在说明峡谷边缘的浪能被弯折进入峡谷时，作用是帮助听者理解额外浪能如何被导入同一通道，从而进一步增强波浪能量。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-246-q04: Nazare峡谷一方面在深水中把能量持续汇聚而不被海底减速，另一方面在靠岸最窄处又因海底几乎垂直上升而把能量向上发射出去，因此可推断它既保持浪能集中，又帮助在近岸把浪抬升成巨浪。

public.prompt: What is the talk mainly about?

options.text: How several animals recover from muscle-related collapses
Why humans can faint from fear while other animals do not
Why horror films are more dangerous than real threats
How hospital treatments for fainting injuries have improved

evidence.quote: in humans, once the threat is over, our body sometimes overcompensates, dropping our blood pressure too low and reducing the blood supply to our brain, causing us to briefly lose consciousness - in other words, we faint. While a few other animals do fall over, mainly from unrelated muscle issues, we're the only ones who lose consciousness.

private.explanationZh: 这道题考查全文主旨。讲者先说明人类在危险过后有时会因血压过度下降而短暂失去意识，随后又对比说其他动物即使会倒下，也通常不是这种失去意识的情况，所以核心话题就是解释为什么人类会因惊吓而昏厥，而其他动物不会。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。文中只顺带提到少数动物会因肌肉问题倒下，并不是在讨论它们如何恢复。
optionRationalesZh.b: 正确。它准确概括了讲座的中心：解释人类因惊吓昏厥的原因以及这种现象为何在人类中独特。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。恐怖片只是后面用来举例说明诱因之一，不是全文主旨。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。文中提到昏厥会导致住院，但没有介绍医院如何治疗。

public.prompt: According to the talk, why does standing upright make fainting more likely in humans?

options.text: It forces blood to work against gravity to reach the brain.
It makes the heart pump less blood during emotional events.
It causes the legs to use less blood when people are moving.
It prevents the brain from demanding extra oxygen.

evidence.quote: Spending most of our time upright, with our brain above our heart, means our blood has to fight gravity to reach the brain.

private.explanationZh: 这道细节题直接对应讲者对直立姿势的解释。人类的大脑位于心脏上方，因此血液要逆着重力往上输送，一旦血压下降，供血就更容易不足，从而更容易昏厥。

optionRationalesZh.a: 正确。原文明确说血液必须“对抗重力”才能到达大脑，这正是直立姿势增加风险的原因。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。文中没有说情绪事件会让心脏泵血减少，反而一开始血压会上升。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。原文说久站时腿部会聚集大量血液，不是说运动时腿部用血更少。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。文中说人脑对血液需求很高，并非直立姿势阻止大脑需要氧气。

public.prompt: What can be inferred about why other animals usually do not faint when their blood pressure drops?

options.text: Their bodies are unable to change blood pressure after a threat ends.
They immediately lie down whenever they feel emotional stress.
Their brains can keep functioning with a lower blood supply than human brains can.
Their muscle problems protect them from losing consciousness.

evidence.quote: while even a significant drop in other animals' blood pressure won't stop their brains from getting enough blood to keep running or hopping.

private.explanationZh: 这道推断题要求根据对比关系得出结论。讲者说，即使其他动物血压明显下降，它们的大脑仍能得到足够血液继续运转，因此可以推断，它们的大脑对供血下降的承受能力通常比人类更强。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。文中没有说其他动物不会调节血压，只是说血压下降未必让它们昏厥。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。原文没有提到其他动物在情绪压力下会立刻躺下。
optionRationalesZh.c: 正确。虽然原文没有直接用“承受能力更强”这几个字，但“仍能得到足够血液继续运转”正支持这一推断。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。肌肉问题只是解释少数动物会倒下的另一种原因，不是防止失去意识的保护机制。

public.prompt: What does the speaker imply about the traits that make humans faint from fright?

options.text: They evolved mainly to protect humans from predators.
They are found only in modern societies with hospitals.
They became useful only after humans started watching horror films.
They are tied to the same characteristics that helped humans thrive widely.

evidence.quote: big brain that make humans the only animals to faint from fright are the same traits that have allowed us to thrive and spread all over the globe.

private.explanationZh: 结尾处的意思是，人类容易因惊吓昏厥并不是单纯的“设计缺陷”。造成这一现象的两大特征，直立姿势和大脑发达，也正是帮助人类成功扩散并繁盛的关键条件。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。文中没有说这些特征是为了防御捕食者而演化出来的。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。医院只是说明昏厥后果严重，不是这些特征存在的条件。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。恐怖片只是现代例子，不是这些特征产生作用的唯一场景。
optionRationalesZh.d: 正确。它准确概括了结尾的反讽式总结：导致昏厥的特征同时也带来了人类的成功。

reasonsZh.minute-earth-247-q01: 全文都在解释人类为什么会因惊吓而晕厥，以及其他动物为什么通常不会，主旨对应b。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-247-q02: 原文明确说直立姿势使大脑位于心脏上方，血液必须对抗重力到达大脑，所以更容易因供血不足而晕倒。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-247-q03: 讲者说明其他动物即使血压明显下降，大脑仍能获得足够血液继续运作，可推知其大脑对较低供血的耐受性高于人类。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-247-q04: 结尾明确指出让人类会因惊吓昏厥的直立姿势和大脑，也是帮助人类繁盛并扩散全球的特征，因此应选d。

public.prompt: What is the main purpose of the talk?

options.text: To compare the mating habits of birds, reptiles, and mammals
To show that embryo development always repeats adult evolution
To explain evidence that amniote penises share a common evolutionary origin
To describe why some modern animals have the most complex penises

evidence.quote: did the amniote penis arise multiple times or did it arise just once?
That nub was powerful proof of a common penis genesis in amniotes; one ancestor passed along to all of us the same penis-building blueprint.

private.explanationZh: 这道主旨题需要把开头提出的核心问题和后文给出的答案结合起来看。讲者先问羊膜动物的阴茎是多次独立演化还是只起源一次，随后用胚胎证据说明它们共享一个共同起源，因此全文目的就是解释支持共同起源的证据。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。文中虽然提到不同动物，但重点不是比较交配习性。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。胚胎发育只是证据来源之一，讲者并没有提出“发育总会重演成体进化”的绝对说法。
optionRationalesZh.c: 正确。它既包含了开头的研究问题，也概括了后文给出的核心结论。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。复杂形态只是引入话题的背景，不是整段讲解的主要目的。

public.prompt: Which two evolutionary explanations does the speaker contrast at the start of the talk?

options.text: Loss during development and regrowth after hatching
Independent origins in different lineages and a single shared origin
Sexual selection and natural selection against mating
Changes in tails and changes in limbs

evidence.quote: The first possibility is known as convergent evolution: this cornucopia of copulatory contraptions may have arisen independently in different branches of the tree.

private.explanationZh: 这道细节题考查开头对两种解释框架的对比。原文先介绍“趋同演化”，即不同支系各自独立演化出阴茎；与之相对的另一种解释则是所有羊膜动物的阴茎只出现过一次，之后在后代中保留、变化或丢失。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。鸟类胚胎中结构后期退化是后文证据，不是开头对比的两大解释。
optionRationalesZh.b: 正确。它准确对应讲者对“多次独立起源”和“单一起源”的对比。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。文中讨论的是器官起源史，不是两种选择压力之间的对立。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。尾巴只是类比材料，并不是被对比的两种关于阴茎起源的解释。

public.prompt: What can be inferred from the evidence about bird embryos and tuatara embryos?

options.text: They support the idea that amniotes inherited the same basic developmental blueprint from a common ancestor.
They show that adult birds probably had larger penises in the recent past.
They prove tuataras are more closely related to mammals than to reptiles.
They suggest embryonic structures usually have no evolutionary significance.

evidence.quote: That nub was powerful proof of a common penis genesis in amniotes; one ancestor passed along to all of us the same penis-building blueprint.

private.explanationZh: 这道推断题要从胚胎中的“残留结构”推出更大的演化结论。讲者把鸟类和喙头蜥胚胎中都出现的阴茎芽视为强有力证据，说明它们共享同一个祖先传下来的发育蓝图，而不是各支系各自重新发明。

optionRationalesZh.a: 正确。原文直接把这种胚胎证据解释为共同起源和共同蓝图的证明。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。证据说明的是深层共同起源，不是近代成鸟阴茎大小变化。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。文中没有重新安排这些类群的亲缘关系，更没有说喙头蜥更接近哺乳动物。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。讲者恰恰把这些胚胎结构当作有重要演化意义的证据。

public.prompt: Why does the speaker mention humans, elephants, some whales, and some insects at the end of the transcript?

options.text: To show that all large animals follow the same reproductive strategy as humans
To identify the groups most likely to lose the penis entirely in adulthood
To argue that insects and mammals evolved reproductive organs from different tissues
To illustrate species whose relatively simple penises are associated with more steps before mating

evidence.quote: Penises that are fairly featureless tend to occur more in species like humans, elephants, some whales, and even some insects that go through lots of steps before they engage in actual physical contact.

private.explanationZh: 这里的列举作用是举例说明一种模式：在交配前会经历较多亲密建立步骤的物种，其阴茎往往相对“朴素”或缺少复杂结构。讲者借这些熟悉或多样的例子来支撑这一概括。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。讲者没有说所有大型动物都和人类采取相同繁殖策略。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。这里讨论的是形态较简单，不是说这些类群最可能在成体阶段完全失去阴茎。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。文中没有把这些例子用于论证不同类群的器官来自不同组织。
optionRationalesZh.d: 正确。它准确说出了这些物种被提到的功能：作为支持性例子。

reasonsZh.minute-earth-248-q01: 这段话核心是在比较两种假说，并用胚胎证据说明羊膜动物的阴茎有共同起源，主旨最符合c。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-248-q02: 开头直接对比的是两种解释：不同谱系各自独立演化，或所有羊膜动物共享一次起源，因此答案是b。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-248-q03: 无阴茎的鸟类胚胎和喙头蜥胚胎都出现阴茎芽，支持它们从共同祖先继承了相同的发育蓝图。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-248-q04: 结尾提到人类、象、某些鲸和昆虫，是为了举例说明相对简单、缺少复杂结构的阴茎常见于交配前有更多步骤的物种。

public.prompt: What is the speaker mainly discussing?

options.text: How factories became the main source of air pollution after lockdowns
Why remote work permanently eliminated traffic-related pollution
How hospitals measured global nitrogen dioxide levels
Why indoor air quality could worsen during the pandemic even as outdoor air improved

evidence.quote: As a result, outdoor air quality improved: five months in, the amount of particulate matter and nitrogen dioxide dropped by 31% and 60% globally. But most of us weren't out and about in the fresh-er air.

private.explanationZh: 主旨在于对比“室外更干净”和“室内可能更脏”这两个方向。讲者先说封锁后室外空气有所改善，紧接着转折指出人们更多待在室内，而室内污染物更容易积累，因此全文是在解释疫情期间为何室内空气质量反而可能恶化。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。工厂停工只是室外空气改善的背景之一，不是全文重点。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。文中并没有说远程办公永久消除了交通污染。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。讲者引用了全球污染变化数据，但没有讨论医院如何测量这些指标。
optionRationalesZh.d: 正确。它概括了全文最核心的对比关系和论点。

public.prompt: According to the talk, why can pollutants be more concentrated indoors than outdoors?

options.text: Indoor air is trapped, especially in airtight homes, instead of dispersing.
Indoor pollutants are heavier than outdoor pollutants.
Homes contain more oxygen than streets do.
Rain pushes pollutants from outside into living rooms.

evidence.quote: inside, it's trapped - especially as we build ever-more airtight homes for energy efficiency.

private.explanationZh: 这道细节题直接问室内污染浓度为什么会更高。原文把室外和室内做了对比：室外污染物可以被风和雨带走，而室内空气尤其在密闭住宅中更不容易流通，所以污染物会被困住并逐渐积累。

optionRationalesZh.a: 正确。它正是原文给出的直接原因。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。文中没有说室内污染物“更重”所以更浓。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。氧气含量不是讲者解释浓度差异的原因。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。原文说风雨会帮助室外污染物散去，不是把它们推进室内。

public.prompt: Why does the speaker describe ozone breaking chemical bonds and helping form formaldehyde?

options.text: To argue that ozone is the main cause of viral transmission indoors
To explain why all cleaning products are equally dangerous
To give a concrete example of how cleaning products can create new indoor pollutants
To show that formaldehyde is the only harmful chemical in homes

evidence.quote: For example, ozone in the air can break apart the carbon-carbon bonds in some molecules that make up the cleaning products. The fragments then quickly combine with oxygen or other compounds to form pollutants like formaldehyde.

private.explanationZh: 这里的化学过程不是为了考细节术语，而是为了把前面“清洁反而让空气更脏”这个抽象说法具体化。讲者用臭氧和分子碎片生成甲醛的例子，展示清洁产品如何与环境中的物质反应，产生新的污染物。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。文中讨论的是空气污染，不是病毒传播机制。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。讲者没有说所有清洁产品都同样危险，只是说某些化学成分可能产生有害反应。
optionRationalesZh.c: 正确。这个例子承担的是说明机制、让论点更具体的作用。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。甲醛只是举出的一个污染物例子，不是唯一有害物。

public.prompt: What does the speaker suggest about the long-term effect of the pandemic on indoor air practices?

options.text: It will likely make people stop using filters once the pandemic ends.
It may encourage changes to habits and buildings that improve indoor air quality.
It proves outdoor air pollution is no longer a serious problem.
It guarantees that cancer risks inside homes will disappear.

evidence.quote: the respiratory dangers posed by the COVID pandemic may well be the push we need to make long-overdue changes to our habits and the places we inhabit, which should help us all breathe a little easier.

private.explanationZh: 这道推断题关注结尾的态度和含义。讲者认为，疫情让人们更重视呼吸风险，这种警觉可能推动我们改变日常行为和居住环境，从而带来更长期的室内空气改善。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。后面推荐滤网和净化器，说明讲者并不是在暗示人们会停止使用这些工具。
optionRationalesZh.b: 正确。它准确转述了讲者对长期变化的积极推断。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。文中没有说室外污染问题已经不严重，只是说封锁期间曾短暂改善。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。讲者只是说可能促进改善，并没有保证风险会完全消失。

reasonsZh.minute-earth-249-q01: 讲者先说疫情期间室外空气改善，随后重点转向人们更多待在室内导致室内空气反而可能更差，主旨对应d。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-249-q02: 原文明确对比室外污染物可被风雨带走，而室内空气会被困住，尤其在更密闭的住宅中，因此选a。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-249-q03: 讲者举臭氧打断化学键并形成甲醛，是为了具体说明清洁产品如何反应生成新的室内污染物。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-249-q04: 结尾说疫情带来的呼吸风险也许会推动我们改变习惯和居住环境，从而让呼吸更轻松，说明可能促成改善室内空气的长期变化。

public.prompt: What is the main topic of the talk?

options.text: Why most animals eventually become right-handed through practice
Why having a preferred side can be an efficient and useful arrangement rather than a flaw
How mirror-image limbs always develop identical brain circuits
How humans lost the ability to train both hands equally well

evidence.quote: what's even weirder is why we have a preferred side at all.

private.explanationZh: 这道主旨题要抓住讲者真正要回答的问题。开头先举人和其他动物都有偏侧使用的现象，然后把重点转向“为什么会有偏好侧”，后文围绕效率、神经回路和分工优势展开，所以全文是在解释偏好侧为何可能有益而非只是缺陷。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。文中没有说大多数动物最后都会变成右利手，反而举例说明左右偏好并不一致。
optionRationalesZh.b: 正确。它概括了讲座对“偏好侧为何存在且有何好处”的核心解释。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。讲者强调的是不同侧往往依赖不同回路，不是总会形成完全相同的回路。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。文中没有说人类“失去”了双手同等训练能力，而是讨论那样做的成本与效率。

public.prompt: What can be inferred about why building equal skill in both hands would be costly?

options.text: The stronger hand would interfere with blood flow to the weaker hand.
The brain can control only one limb at a time during learning.
Practice with one hand permanently disables the other hand.
Each hand would need its own separately built and trained neural circuit.

evidence.quote: To write decently with my left hand, because of the quirky 2-sidedness of our brains, I'd need an entirely different circuit, which would take a ton more time and energy to build, operate, and train. Instead, it's much more efficient to rely on a single circuit for writing.

private.explanationZh: 这道推断题基于讲者对大脑左右分工的说明。既然一只手熟练完成任务需要一整套神经回路和训练，那么另一只手若也达到同样水平，就需要再建立、运行并强化另一套系统，因此代价会很高。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。文中从头到尾都在谈神经控制和训练，不涉及手之间的血流竞争。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。讲者没有说学习时大脑一次只能控制一条肢体。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。使用一只手不会永久让另一只手失去能力，文中反而强调另一侧也有重要作用。
optionRationalesZh.d: 正确。它是对原文“需要另一套回路、耗费更多时间和能量”的准确推断。

public.prompt: What did the parrot study suggest about animals with strong side preferences?

options.text: They performed better, especially on more complicated food-finding tasks.
They depended less on coordinated movements than other birds.
They solved simple tasks quickly but failed on difficult ones.
They avoided using their unpreferred side for any task at all.

evidence.quote: the strongly-sided birds did twice as well. What's more, when the task got harder and the birds had to use a series of coordinated motions to get a snack hanging from a string, the strongly sided birds were way better at figuring out a clever solution.

private.explanationZh: 这道细节题直接考查鹦鹉实验的结果。讲者说偏侧更明显的鸟在找食测试中表现翻倍，而在更复杂、需要一连串协调动作的任务中，它们更擅长想出巧妙办法，因此结论是强偏侧与更好的任务表现相关。

optionRationalesZh.a: 正确。它完整概括了简单任务和更复杂任务中的优势。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。恰恰相反，复杂任务需要一系列协调动作，强偏侧鸟表现更好。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。原文说它们在任务变难时依然更出色，不是失败。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。讲者后面明确指出非偏好侧仍有重要的支持性工作。

public.prompt: What point does the speaker make about the so-called weaker side by the end of the talk?

options.text: It becomes strong only in species that use tools.
It exists mainly to make people switch hands during difficult tasks.
It is specialized for complementary supporting tasks rather than being useless.
It is a temporary feature that disappears with enough training.

evidence.quote: Without it, we wouldn't be able to hammer in a nail, play the guitar, catch and throw a baseball, perform heart surgery, or deftly wield a sword and shield.

private.explanationZh: 结尾这里不是在说“弱侧”没用，而是在强调它承担配合性的关键任务。讲者列举双手协作的活动，说明所谓较弱的一侧其实是为不同功能优化的，没有它，许多复杂动作都无法完成。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。文中列举的例子不限于工具使用物种，也没有提出这种条件限制。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。讲者并没有说它存在的主要目的是让人换手，而是让双侧能分工合作。
optionRationalesZh.c: 正确。它抓住了结尾论点：所谓弱侧是互补分工的一部分。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。原文没有说这种侧化会随着训练而消失。

reasonsZh.minute-earth-250-q01: 整段话都在解释为什么偏侧性是一种高效且有益的安排，而不是缺陷，b最能概括主旨。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-250-q02: 讲者明确说若左手也要像右手那样熟练，就需要另一套独立神经回路，并花费更多时间和能量去建立、运作和训练。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-250-q03: 鹦鹉实验表明，偏侧性强的个体在找食物测试中表现更好，任务更复杂时尤其更擅长找到巧妙解法。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-250-q04: 结尾指出所谓较弱的一侧并非没用，而是被优化来承担配合性、支撑性的不同任务，因此应选c。

public.prompt: What is the main point of the talk?

options.text: There is no universally agreed-upon way to define what counts as a tree.
Trees can be identified easily once their height is measured.
Taxonomists classify all woody plants into a single tree group.
Most plants people call trees are actually shrubs.

evidence.quote: there's no definitive way to tell what's a tree and what isn't.

private.explanationZh: 这道主旨题考查全文的核心结论。讲者从日常直觉出发，先后检验形态特征和分类学两条路，最后都发现无法给“树”下一个放之四海而皆准的定义，因此中心观点就是“树”没有公认统一的严格定义。

optionRationalesZh.a: 正确。它最准确地概括了整段讲述的核心发现。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。文中明确说明高度并不能可靠地区分树与灌木。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。讲者正是说明分类学也无法把所有所谓的树归成一个单一群体。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。讲者没有得出“大多数树其实是灌木”这种结论。

public.prompt: Why does the speaker mention palm trees and banana trees?

options.text: To argue that fibrous trunks are evolutionarily older than wooden ones
To prove that bananas and palms belong to the same taxonomic group
To show that a common physical definition of trees excludes some familiar examples
To suggest that height is the only reliable way to define trees

evidence.quote: A palm tree, for example, is tall and has a trunk, but that trunk isn't made out of the hard, highly-structured nutrient-transporting cells we commonly call "wood".

private.explanationZh: 讲者提到棕榈和香蕉，并不是为了介绍这两类植物本身，而是为了拆解人们最自然的“树=高大单干木质植物”定义。它们看起来很像树，却不符合“真正木质”的标准，因此暴露出日常定义的漏洞。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。文中没有比较纤维质树干和木质树干的演化先后。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。这里不是为了证明两者属于同一分类群。
optionRationalesZh.c: 正确。它准确说明这些例子被提出的修辞作用：挑战常见定义。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。讲者实际上是在说明仅靠高度并不可靠。

public.prompt: How does the speaker organize the central argument of the talk?

options.text: By listing tree species from most typical to least typical
By showing that both physical traits and taxonomy fail to produce a universal tree definition
By comparing how forests are classified in different countries
By arguing first from genetics and then from climate change data

evidence.quote: taxonomy isn't helpful, and physical characteristics aren't enough.

private.explanationZh: 这道结构题关注论证推进方式。前半段，讲者用棕榈、香蕉、矮柏和白杨等例子说明外形标准不稳定；后半段再转向分类学，发现亲缘关系同样不能把“树”圈成一个整齐的群。这个句子正把两条论证线收束到一起。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。讲者没有按“典型程度”罗列树种。
optionRationalesZh.b: 正确。它准确概括了全文从两种定义路径入手、最后都失败的结构。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。文中完全没有比较不同国家如何划分森林。
optionRationalesZh.d: 错误。虽然提到遗传关系，但气候变化数据只是后面的赞助关联内容，不是中心论证结构。

public.prompt: What does the speaker suggest at the end of the talk about dealing with natural categories such as "tree"?

options.text: Scientists should stop classifying plants that do not fit everyday language.
Language problems can be solved once enough carbon-offset data are collected.
The best solution is to classify plants only by whether they reduce climate change.
People may need to tolerate some uncertainty instead of forcing a perfectly neat definition.

evidence.quote: maybe it's good for us to be able to tolerate a bit of uncertainty.

private.explanationZh: 结尾的态度并不是放弃研究，而是承认自然和语言本来就可能带有模糊性。讲者暗示，与其强行把“树”塞进一个绝对整齐的定义里，不如接受某些自然类别本身就存在边界模糊。

optionRationalesZh.a: 错误。讲者没有主张停止分类研究，只是说分类未必能给出完美答案。
optionRationalesZh.b: 错误。碳补偿是后面赞助内容，不能解决概念定义问题。
optionRationalesZh.c: 错误。讲者没有建议按气候功能来重新定义树。
optionRationalesZh.d: 正确。它准确转述了结尾对“不确定性”的积极容忍态度。

reasonsZh.minute-earth-251-q01: 讲者反复说明无论按外形还是分类学，都没有公认且普适的“树”定义，因此主旨是a。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-251-q02: 提到棕榈和香蕉树，是为了说明人们常见的物理定义如“高大、单干、木质”会把一些大家熟悉的树排除在外。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-251-q03: 论证结构先展示外形特征定义会失败，再说明分类学也无法给出统一定义，所以b准确概括了组织方式。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-251-q04: 结尾说自然和语言本来就很混乱，也许我们需要容忍一点不确定性，而不是强行追求完全整齐的定义。

public.prompt: What is the main topic of the talk?

options.text: Why animal survival curves cannot be used for humans
How human survivorship has changed over time
Why old age is biologically new for modern humans
How to predict the exact lifespan of individuals

evidence.quote: how human survival has changed over time. So we dug into the data to create survivorship curves for humans throughout history.

private.explanationZh: 开头直接点明主题不是单纯讨论现代人的寿命数字，而是追踪人类存活曲线在历史中的变化。因此正确答案是“人类存活模式如何随时间改变”。

optionRationalesZh.a: 文中确实提到动物曲线，但那只是比较工具，不是核心主题。
optionRationalesZh.b: 正确，讲话者明确说他们要研究人类在历史上的 survivorship curves。
optionRationalesZh.c: 文中反而强调“getting old isn't new”，所以这个说法与主旨相反。
optionRationalesZh.d: 全文没有讨论如何精确预测个人寿命，而是讨论群体存活模式。

public.prompt: Why does the speaker mention John Adams?

options.text: To argue that life expectancy in the 1800s was calculated incorrectly
To suggest that only presidents could survive to old age
To show that some people still lived very long lives despite a low average
To prove that disease was less serious in earlier centuries

evidence.quote: In the early 1800's, the average life expectancy of white men in the US was only 42 - yet U.S. President John Adams was 90 when he died in 1826. It's not like, back then, men in their 30s were senior citizens

private.explanationZh: 讲话者用 John Adams 活到 90 岁这个例子，说明平均寿命低并不等于人人都活不到老年。真正的问题是很多年轻人早逝，把平均值拉低了。

optionRationalesZh.a: 文中没有说统计方法错误，而是说平均值会被年轻死亡人数扭曲。
optionRationalesZh.b: John Adams 只是举例，不是在说只有总统才能长寿。
optionRationalesZh.c: 正确，这个例子正是为了反驳“过去没人活得久”的误解。
optionRationalesZh.d: 文中后面明确说战争和疾病导致许多年轻人死亡，所以这项推断错误。

public.prompt: According to the talk, what mainly explains why many more people now survive from age 60 to age 80?

options.text: The development of drugs that fight pneumonia
A genetic mutation that improves DNA repair
Better records kept by wealthy families
A sharp decline in deaths from war during youth

evidence.quote: in modern humans, it's mostly thanks to the development of drugs that fight pneumonia, which used to be the number one killer of old folks.

private.explanationZh: 这里的细节很直接：现代人晚年生存率上升，主要不是因为像鲸那样的 DNA 修复突变，而是因为能对抗肺炎的药物发展起来了。

optionRationalesZh.a: 正确，原文明确说现代人的晚年长寿“mostly thanks to”治疗肺炎的药物。
optionRationalesZh.b: 这是鲸长寿的可能原因，不是现代人类晚年生存率提升的主要原因。
optionRationalesZh.c: 记录更完整只影响数据质量，不会真正提高人们活到 80 岁的概率。
optionRationalesZh.d: 青年时期战争减少与这一句谈的“60 到 80 岁的存活”不是文中给出的主要解释。

public.prompt: According to the talk, which development helped sharply reduce infant mortality?

options.text: Longer presidential leadership
Aging more like domestic dogs
Keeping more records for the rich
Wider access to hospital care, vaccinations, and clean water

evidence.quote: But 200 years later, thanks to breakthroughs like widespread access to hospital care, childhood vaccinations, and clean drinking water, very few white male American babies died.

private.explanationZh: 讲话者把婴儿死亡率下降归因于公共卫生和医疗突破，尤其是医院照护、儿童疫苗接种和清洁饮水。这是末段最重要的具体支持细节之一。

optionRationalesZh.a: 总统任期与婴儿死亡率下降没有因果关系，文中也没有这样说。
optionRationalesZh.b: 这是前面用于类比成人中年存活的比喻，不是婴儿死亡率下降的原因。
optionRationalesZh.c: 保存记录只会影响研究样本，不会降低婴儿死亡率。
optionRationalesZh.d: 正确，原文逐项列出了这些突破。

reasonsZh.minute-earth-252-q01: 开头明确说观众想知道“human survival has changed over time”，后文依次讲19世纪、中期、现代与未来的人类存活曲线变化，主旨就是人类存活状况如何随时间改变。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-252-q02: 说话者先说19世纪早期平均预期寿命只有42岁，接着举John Adams活到90岁的例子，是为了说明平均值低不等于没人长寿，仍有人能活到很老。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-252-q03: 谈到现代人60岁后更常活到80岁时，原文说现代人不像鲸那样靠DNA修复突变，而“mostly thanks to the development of drugs that fight pneumonia”，因此答案是抗肺炎药物的发展。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-252-q04: 婴儿死亡率下降一段明确列出原因：更广泛的医院护理、儿童疫苗接种和清洁饮用水，所以只有选项d完整对应原文。

public.prompt: Why does the speaker mention heating food, water, and surgical equipment?

options.text: To recommend home remedies for infection
To contrast literal germ-killing heat with what fever actually does
To show that humans copied microbes' defense systems
To explain why fevers usually exceed 65 degrees Celsius

evidence.quote: we also heat raw food, questionable water, and used surgical equipment to literally sizzle pathogens to death. But that's not how a fever works.

private.explanationZh: 这组例子先唤起听众对“高温杀菌”的直觉理解，然后马上转折说 fever 并不是这样工作的。它的作用是形成对照，帮助引出真正机制。

optionRationalesZh.a: 讲话者不是在提供家庭治疗建议，而是在建立一个错误直觉后再纠正它。
optionRationalesZh.b: 正确，这些例子用来和 fever 的真实作用形成鲜明对比。
optionRationalesZh.c: 文中没有说人类模仿微生物，只是在讨论免疫机制。
optionRationalesZh.d: 后文恰恰说发烧通常远达不到那种直接烫死病原体的温度。

public.prompt: What is the main point of the talk?

options.text: Fevers mainly damage pathogens and human cells in equal measure
Pathogens rely on heat shock proteins to resist temperature changes
The body uses fever only when other immune responses have failed
Fevers mainly help by strengthening the body's own immune response

evidence.quote: A few extra degrees can slow down the replication of certain pathogens, but the bigger effect of a fever is how it benefits our own bodies.

private.explanationZh: 全文核心论点是：发烧不是靠高温直接“烧死”病菌，而是通过帮助免疫细胞更快、更有效地工作来保护身体。这个句子最集中地概括了主旨。

optionRationalesZh.a: 文中明确否定了“高温直接杀病原体”是主要机制的理解。
optionRationalesZh.b: 热休克蛋白确实出现了，但主旨不是病原体如何抗热，而是宿主如何借此增强防御。
optionRationalesZh.c: 原文没有说 fever 只在其他免疫反应失败后才出现。
optionRationalesZh.d: 正确，这正是作者反复说明的核心观点。

public.prompt: According to the talk, how do heat shock proteins help T cells?

options.text: They help T cells clump together and reach lymph nodes more efficiently
They lower body temperature so T cells are not damaged
They convert pathogens directly into harmless molecules
They keep T cells inside the bone marrow until antibodies form

evidence.quote: They bind to the tails of T cells - and then bind several T cells' tails together - helping the cells clump up, grab blood vessel walls, and make the trek to the lymph nodes

private.explanationZh: 这道题考查中段关键机制细节。热休克蛋白帮助 T 细胞彼此聚集、抓住血管壁，并更快到达淋巴结，从而及时识别病原体并组织反应。

optionRationalesZh.a: 正确，原文逐步说明了 clump up、grab blood vessel walls 和去 lymph nodes 的过程。
optionRationalesZh.b: 热休克蛋白不是通过降温起作用，全文讨论的是升温触发保护过程。
optionRationalesZh.c: 它们可以帮助递送病原体相关分子，但这里对 T 细胞的作用不是“直接转化病原体”。
optionRationalesZh.d: 文中说 T 细胞要更快赶到淋巴结，而不是被留在骨髓里。

public.prompt: What can be inferred about raising body temperature as a defense?

options.text: It works only in mammals that control body temperature internally
It matters mainly because it instantly kills invading germs
It is an ancient strategy used widely across different kinds of animals
It evolved recently along with modern medical treatment

evidence.quote: Even critters that can't regulate their body temperature internally will bask, buoy, or buzz until they're toasty - and infection-free.

private.explanationZh: 结尾把这种策略扩展到不能内温调节的动物，说明“升温抗感染”并不是少数哺乳动物才有的特殊做法，而是跨物种、历史悠久的防御方式。

optionRationalesZh.a: 原文专门说连不能内部调温的生物也会靠外部方式升温，所以这项错误。
optionRationalesZh.b: 全文一直在纠正“直接烫死病菌”这个误解。
optionRationalesZh.c: 正确，结合结尾与前文“600 million years ago”可推知这是古老而广泛的策略。
optionRationalesZh.d: 文中把这一机制追溯到远古祖先，不是现代医学时代才出现。

reasonsZh.minute-earth-253-q01: 前面先提加热食物、水和器械可以直接“sizzle pathogens to death”，紧接着就说“that's not how a fever works”，这是用来对比真正高温杀菌与发烧的实际机制。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-253-q02: 整段核心是在解释发烧并不是靠直接烫死病原体，而是主要通过增强中性粒细胞、T细胞和热休克蛋白等机体自身防御来帮助恢复，因此主旨是发烧强化免疫反应。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-253-q03: 原文明确说热休克蛋白会结合T细胞尾部，把几个T细胞连在一起，帮助它们成团、抓住血管壁并前往淋巴结，所以a与文本一致。
reasonsZh.minute-earth-253-q04: 后文说祖先早在6亿年前就利用这种机制，且不能体内调温的动物也会通过晒太阳、漂浮或振翅升温来对抗感染，可推知升温防御是跨多类动物、非常古老的策略。

public.prompt: What explains why grizzly bears often leave much of a salmon uneaten?

options.text: Bears often leave much of a salmon because the richest parts are quickest to process
Bears abandon salmon mainly because they forget where they left them
Salmon flesh has too little nutrition for any predator to survive on
Grizzly bears catch extra fish mainly to store them for winter

evidence.quote: For a bear in salmon season, fish is easy to come by; dismantling that fish is what takes the real time and energy.

private.explanationZh: 整篇讲的是“为什么熊看似浪费食物，其实很合算”。核心逻辑是捕到鱼并不难，真正耗时的是处理鱼，所以熊会优先吃最值钱、最容易取到的部分。

optionRationalesZh.a: 正确，这一句概括了全文主线：关键成本在处理而不在捕获。
optionRationalesZh.b: 文中把行为解释为策略选择，不是遗忘。
optionRationalesZh.c: 讲话者说剩下的肉较瘦、回报较低，不是说完全没有营养。
optionRationalesZh.d: 全文没有提到它们这么做是为了储存过冬食物。

public.prompt: Which salmon parts does the speaker describe as especially worthwhile for bears to eat?

options.text: The tail, spine, and fins
The gills, liver, and scales
The bones, muscles, and stomach
The skin, the brain, and the eggs

evidence.quote: The fattiest bits of the fish - the skin, the brain, and the eggs - are the most calorie rich and easiest to access.

private.explanationZh: 这是一个直接细节题。讲话者明确点出最值得吃的是脂肪最丰富、又最容易获取的部分：皮、脑和鱼卵。

optionRationalesZh.a: 这些部位没有被列为最划算的部分。
optionRationalesZh.b: 原文并未提到这些器官是熊优先选择的高回报部分。
optionRationalesZh.c: 这些更接近难处理的剩余部分，不是讲话者强调的最佳选择。
optionRationalesZh.d: 正确，原文逐字列出了这三项。

public.prompt: Why does the speaker mention pelicans?

options.text: To show that all predators prefer the fattiest parts of prey
To contrast animals that spend more time searching than processing food
To argue that birds are more efficient hunters than bears
To explain why fish are harder to catch than to eat

evidence.quote: Meanwhile, a pelican - which spends far more time searching for fish than eating them - will eat pretty much everything it can catch.
